summary: Gruber argues that the PC hardware industry suffers from a pathological herd mentality, rooted in its dependence on Microsoft Windows. He traces this conformity from old arguments that Apple should license Mac OS or switch to Windows, through netbook punditry, to the current state where PC makers cannot differentiate because they all ship the same OS. He contends that Apple's ability to innovate stems directly from controlling both hardware and software, making it the only PC maker that can be genuinely different. The piece concludes that the industry would be better served if more companies could move the state of the art forward independently.
key_points: The old arguments that Apple should license Mac OS or make Windows PCs were both expressions of the same conformist instinct — that Apple should stop being different
PC hardware makers are fundamentally crippled by their dependence on Microsoft Windows, unable to differentiate even if they wanted to
Apple's hardware innovation is inextricably tied to its control of its own operating system, with the iPhone as the strongest example
Companies like Dell, Sony, and HP should be embarrassed that their computers ship with the same Windows as no-name brands
Google Chrome OS might free PC makers from Microsoft but won't help them differentiate from each other
money_quotes: You have to be different to be better, but different is scary.
Apple's ability to produce innovative hardware is inextricably intertwined with its ability to produce innovative software.
It's not just that Apple is different among computer makers. It's that Apple is the only one that even can be different, because it's the only one that has its own OS.
Part of the industry-wide herd mentality is an assumption that no one else can make a computer OS — that anyone can make a computer but only Microsoft can make an OS.

It should be embarrassing to companies like Dell and Sony, with deep pockets and strong brand names, that they're stuck selling computers with the same copy of Windows installed as the no-name brands.
Operating systems aren't mere components like RAM or CPUs; they're the single most important part of the computing experience.
Chrome OS might help PC makers break free of Microsoft, but it won't help them break free from each other.
key_insight: Apple's unique position in the industry comes not just from choosing to be different, but from being the only PC maker that controls its own operating system — the single most important part of the computing experience.
